warningLines.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Bg5 e6 f4 Be7 Qf3 Qc7 O-O-O Nbd7 g4 b5 Bxf6 Nxf6 g5 Nd7 f5 Nc5 f6 gxf6 gxf6 Bf8 Bh3
warningLines.explanation: In the 6.Bg5 lines, the f5-f6 break is devastating.

openings.name: Grunfeld Defense (Firouzja)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Nf3 c5 Rb1 O-O Be2 cxd4 cxd4 Qa5+ Bd2 Qxa2 O-O Bg4
openings.overview: Firouzja uses the Grunfeld to fight for a win with Black against 1.d4. The opening allows White to build a massive center, which Black then attacks with piece pressure. It suits his dynamic, fearless style perfectly.
keyIdeas: Allow White a big center and then undermine it with c5
The Bg7 exerts tremendous pressure along the long diagonal
Grab the a2 pawn when possible — it is a real material gain
Play for activity and tactical complications
traps: After bxc3 Bg7 Nf3 c5 Rb1, if White plays Be3, Black can grab a2 with Qa5+ Bd2 Qxa2
warnings: Do not let White consolidate the center without counterplay
The a2 pawn grab can be dangerous if White gets too much initiative

variations.name: Exchange Variation with Qa5+
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Nf3 c5 Rb1 O-O Be2 cxd4 cxd4 Qa5+ Bd2 Qxa2 O-O Bg4
variations.explanation: Firouzja's aggressive approach. Black grabs the a-pawn and creates immediate pressure. The resulting positions are razor-sharp.

variations.name: Russian System with Qb3
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Bg7 Qb3 dxc4 Qxc4 O-O e4 a6 Be2 b5 Qb3 c5 dxc5 Be6 Qc2 Nbd7
variations.explanation: Against the Russian System, Firouzja plays b5 and c5 to challenge White's center. The position offers rich play for both sides.

trapLines.name: a2 Pawn Grab Danger
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Nf3 c5 Rb1 O-O Be2 cxd4 cxd4 Qa5+ Bd2 Qxa2 O-O Bg4 Bg5 Nc6 d5 Na5 Rxb7 Nc4 Bc1
trapLines.explanation: Grabbing the a2 pawn is risky. After Rxb7 and d5, White's pieces are incredibly active.

trapLines.name: Exchange Variation Pressure
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Bc4 O-O Ne2 c5 O-O Nc6 Be3 Bg4 f3 Na5 Bxf7+ Rxf7 fxg4 Rxf1+ Kxf1 cxd4 cxd4 Qb6
trapLines.explanation: Bc4 targets f7 with potential sacrifice.

warningLines.name: Center Consolidation Danger
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Nf3 c5 Be3 Qa5 Qd2 O-O Rc1 cxd4 cxd4 Qxd2+ Nxd2 Rd8 d5 e6 Nc4 exd5 exd5 Na6 Bd4 Bxd4
warningLines.explanation: If White consolidates with d5, the passed pawn ties down Black's pieces.

openings.name: French Defense (Firouzja)
openings.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 dxe4 Nxe4 Be7 Bxf6 gxf6 Nf3 b6 Bc4 Bb7 Qe2 Nd7 O-O-O c5
openings.overview: Firouzja occasionally surprises opponents with the French Defense. It is an unexpected weapon that catches 1.e4 players off guard. The Burn Variation with dxe4 leads to an asymmetric position where Firouzja's creativity can flourish.
keyIdeas: Play e6 to build a solid pawn chain and prepare d5
The French structure is resilient and hard to break down
After dxe4, the position opens up for piece play
The doubled f-pawns after Bxf6 gxf6 give Black central control and open files
traps: In the Winawer with Bb4, if White plays a3 Ba5, the bishop can retreat to c7 and remain active
warnings: The light-squared bishop is traditionally passive — develop it via b7 or a6
Be careful in the Advance Variation — c5 must come quickly

variations.name: Burn Variation 4...dxe4
variations.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 dxe4 Nxe4 Be7 Bxf6 gxf6 Nf3 b6 Bc4 Bb7 Qe2 Nd7 O-O-O c5
variations.explanation: Firouzja's preferred French setup. The doubled f-pawns look ugly but give Black excellent central control. The position is unbalanced and dynamic.

variations.name: Classical 4...Be7
variations.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e5 Nfd7 Bxe7 Qxe7 f4 O-O Nf3 c5 Qd2 Nc6 dxc5 Nxc5 O-O-O f6
variations.explanation: A more traditional approach where Black develops the bishop before it gets locked in. After e5 Nfd7, the position becomes a typical French structure.

trapLines.name: Burn Variation Activity
trapLines.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 dxe4 Nxe4 Be7 Bxf6 gxf6 Nf3 b6 Bc4 Bb7 Qe2 Nd7 O-O-O c5 Nxc5 Nxc5 dxc5 Qxd1+ Rxd1 bxc5 Bxe6 fxe6 Rd7
trapLines.explanation: In the Burn Variation, the doubled f-pawns give central control and open files.

trapLines.name: Winawer Poison Pawn
trapLines.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nc3 Bb4 e5 c5 a3 Bxc3+ bxc3 Ne7 Qg4 Qc7 Qxg7 Rg8 Qxh7 cxd4 Ne2 Nbc6 f4 dxc3 Qd3 d4 Nxd4 Nxd4 Qxd4 Bd7
trapLines.explanation: The Winawer Poison Pawn is one of the sharpest lines in chess.

warningLines.name: Light Bishop Passivity
warningLines.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e5 Nfd7 Bxe7 Qxe7 f4 O-O Nf3 c5 Qd2 Nc6 dxc5 Nxc5 O-O-O f6 exf6 Qxf6 Bd3 Bd7 Rhe1 Rae8 Kb1 Kh8
warningLines.explanation: In the Classical French, Black's light-squared bishop is the eternal problem piece.

openings.name: Catalan Opening (Dubov)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bd2 Be4 Qc1 Nc6
openings.overview: Dubov plays the Catalan with his trademark creativity. While most players use it as a slow grinding weapon, Dubov finds unexpected tactical resources and dynamic ideas. His Catalan games are always surprising and entertaining.
keyIdeas: The Bg2 creates long-term pressure along the long diagonal
Recover the c4 pawn with Qc2 or a4 while maintaining pressure
Look for dynamic piece play rather than just positional grinding
Use unexpected pawn sacrifices to generate initiative
traps: If Black tries to hold c4 with b5, the Bg2 becomes a monster and a4 undermines the structure
warnings: Do not play on autopilot — the Catalan requires awareness of tactical possibilities
Respect Black's counterplay with c5 — it can equalize if timed well

variations.name: Open Catalan
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bd2 Be4 Qc1 Nc6
variations.explanation: Dubov's main Catalan line. He recovers the pawn with Qc2 and develops the queenside actively. The Bd2 move prepares Bc3 with kingside pressure.

variations.name: Closed Catalan with e4
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O Nbd7 Qc2 c6 Nbd2 b6 e4 Bb7 e5 Ne8 Re1 Nc7
variations.explanation: A more aggressive approach where Dubov pushes e4-e5. The position becomes sharp and White has kingside attacking chances.

trapLines.name: b5 Pawn Grab Punished
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 a4 Bd7 Qxc4 Bc6 Bf4 Nbd7 Nc3 Nb6 Qb3 Nbd5 Nxd5 Nxd5 Be5 f6 Bd6 Bxd6 Qxd5
trapLines.explanation: In the Catalan, holding the c4 pawn is very difficult. White recovers material with interest.

trapLines.name: Bg2 Diagonal Dominance
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 Be7 O-O O-O Qc2 a6 a4 Bd7 Qxc4 Bc6 Bf4 Nbd7 Nc3 Nb6 Qb3 Nbd5 Nxd5 Nxd5 Be5 f6 Bd6
trapLines.explanation: The Bg2 dominates the long diagonal. After capturing on a8, White's material advantage is decisive.

warningLines.name: c5 Equalizing Break
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bf4 Nd5 Bd2 c5 dxc5 Bxc5 Nc3 Nxc3 Bxc3 Nd7 Rfd1 Qe7 Ng5 Nf6
warningLines.explanation: Black's c5 break releases the tension and gives active play.

openings.name: Anti-Marshall Ruy Lopez (Dubov)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O h3 Bb7 d3 d6 a3 Na5 Ba2 c5 Nbd2 Nc6
openings.overview: Dubov plays the Anti-Marshall Ruy Lopez to avoid the sharp Marshall Gambit while keeping dynamic chances. His deep preparation in these quiet-looking positions often catches opponents by surprise when tactical complications arise from seemingly calm structures.
keyIdeas: Avoid the Marshall Attack with h3 and d3
Build a solid center and prepare a slow kingside expansion
The a3 and Ba2 setup secures the bishop and prepares b4
Look for central breaks with d4 or c3-d4 when ready
traps: If Black plays d5 prematurely, exd5 Nxd5 gives White pressure on e5 and the open e-file
warnings: Do not rush d4 without preparation — the center must be ready
Watch for a5-a4 pawn breaks that target the b3/a2 bishop diagonal

variations.name: h3 Anti-Marshall
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O h3 Bb7 d3 d6 a3 Na5 Ba2 c5 Nbd2 Nc6
variations.explanation: Dubov's preferred Anti-Marshall setup. The h3-d3-a3 triangle is solid and flexible. White can expand with c3-d4 when the time is right.

variations.name: d3 Slow System
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 d3 b5 Bb3 d6 a4 Bd7 c3 O-O Nbd2 Na5 Ba2 c5 Re1 Nc6
variations.explanation: An even quieter approach where White plays d3 before Re1. The position builds slowly but White retains long-term chances.

trapLines.name: d5 Premature Break
trapLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O h3 Bb7 d3 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Rxe5 Nf4 Re3 Nxh3+ gxh3 Qd7 Nc3 Qxh3 Nd5 Bf6
trapLines.explanation: If Black plays d5 prematurely, White captures with strong e-file pressure.

trapLines.name: b4 Queenside Expansion
trapLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O h3 Bb7 d3 d6 a3 Na5 Ba2 c5 Nbd2 Nc6 Nf1 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Ng3 Bf6 Bd2
trapLines.explanation: White expands on the queenside with a3 and b4.

warningLines.name: a5-a4 Counter Pressure
warningLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O h3 Bb7 d3 d6 a3 Na5 Ba2 c5 Nbd2 Nc6 c3 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Nf1 f6 Ng3 Qd7 d4 exd4 cxd4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nxd4
warningLines.explanation: Black can generate counterplay with a5-a4, targeting the bishop diagonal.

openings.name: Tarrasch French (Dubov)
openings.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nd2 Nf6 e5 Nfd7 Bd3 c5 c3 Nc6 Ne2 cxd4 cxd4 f6 exf6 Nxf6 O-O Bd6 Nf3 O-O
openings.overview: Dubov uses the Tarrasch Variation against the French Defense. By playing 3.Nd2 instead of Nc3, White avoids the Winawer complications and steers toward a positional battle. The resulting positions offer White a space advantage and clear plans.
keyIdeas: Nd2 avoids the Winawer and keeps a solid pawn structure
Build a center with e5 and restrict Black's pieces
After c3 and Ne2, White has a flexible setup for d4 support
The f6 break by Black opens the position but White is better prepared
traps: If Black delays c5 too long, White builds up with Ngf3 and O-O with a dominating center
warnings: Do not play f4 too early — it can leave the e4-e5 chain overextended
Respect the c5 break — it is Black's main source of counterplay

variations.name: Main Line with e5
variations.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nd2 Nf6 e5 Nfd7 Bd3 c5 c3 Nc6 Ne2 cxd4 cxd4 f6 exf6 Nxf6 O-O Bd6 Nf3 O-O
variations.explanation: The standard Tarrasch line. White builds a strong center and develops smoothly. Black must challenge with c5 and f6 to get counterplay.

variations.name: 3...c5 Immediate
variations.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nd2 c5 Ngf3 cxd4 Nxd4 Nc6 Bb5 Bd7 Nxc6 Bxc6 Bxc6+ bxc6 O-O Nf6 e5 Nd7 Nf3 Be7
variations.explanation: When Black plays c5 immediately, the position simplifies. White has a slight edge due to the better pawn structure.

trapLines.name: e5 Space Advantage
trapLines.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nd2 Nf6 e5 Nfd7 Bd3 c5 c3 Nc6 Ne2 cxd4 cxd4 f6 exf6 Nxf6 O-O Bd6 Nf3 O-O Bf4 Bxf4 Nxf4 Qb6 Qd2 Bd7 Rac1 Rac8 Bb5
trapLines.explanation: White's e5 space advantage cramps Black.

trapLines.name: c5 Delayed Error
trapLines.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nd2 c5 Ngf3 cxd4 Nxd4 Nc6 Bb5 Bd7 Nxc6 Bxc6 Bxc6+ bxc6 O-O Nf6 e5 Nd7 Nf3 Be7 Re1 O-O c4 Rb8 Bf4 dxc4 Qa4
trapLines.explanation: If Black plays c5 immediately, the position simplifies with White retaining an edge.

warningLines.name: Black's c5 Counterplay
warningLines.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nd2 Nf6 e5 Nfd7 Bd3 c5 c3 Nc6 Ne2 cxd4 cxd4 f6 exf6 Nxf6 O-O Bd6 Nf3 Qc7 Bg5 O-O Rc1 Bd7 Bb5 a6 Bxc6 Bxc6 Nc3 Bb4
warningLines.explanation: Black's c5 break followed by f6 is the standard counter.

openings.name: Sicilian Sveshnikov (Dubov)
openings.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Ndb5 d6 Bg5 a6 Na3 b5 Nd5 Be7 Bxf6 Bxf6 c4 b4 Nc2 a5
openings.overview: Dubov plays the Sveshnikov with his trademark creativity. He has contributed many original ideas to this opening, particularly in the critical middlegame positions. His willingness to accept structural weaknesses for dynamic play makes the Sveshnikov ideal for his style.
keyIdeas: Push e5 early to gain space and restrict White's knight
Accept the d5 weakness for active piece play and the bishop pair
Play a6 and b5 for queenside counterplay
The f5 break is the key to Black's counterplay in most lines
traps: After Nd5 Be7 Bxf6 Bxf6, Black can play c4 to gain the bishop pair and queenside space
warnings: The d5 square is a permanent weakness — fight for activity to compensate
Do not allow White to blockade on d5 without creating counterplay elsewhere

variations.name: Main Line with c4
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Ndb5 d6 Bg5 a6 Na3 b5 Nd5 Be7 Bxf6 Bxf6 c4 b4 Nc2 a5
variations.explanation: Dubov's favored continuation. The c4 push from White is met with dynamic counterplay on the queenside. Black's bishop pair provides long-term compensation.

variations.name: Chelyabinsk with gxf6
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Ndb5 d6 Bg5 a6 Na3 b5 Bxf6 gxf6 Nd5 Bg7 Bd3 Ne7 Nxe7 Qxe7
variations.explanation: An alternative where Black takes on f6 with the g-pawn. The resulting positions have an unusual structure that Dubov loves to play.

trapLines.name: c4 Space Squeeze
trapLines.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Ndb5 d6 Bg5 a6 Na3 b5 Nd5 Be7 Bxf6 Bxf6 c4 b4 Nc2 a5 g3 O-O Bg2 Bg5 O-O Be6 Nce3 Rb8 a3 bxa3 bxa3
trapLines.explanation: White's c4 push grabs queenside space. Combined with Nd5, the positional bind is hard to break.

trapLines.name: Bg7 Diagonal Pressure
trapLines.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Ndb5 d6 Bg5 a6 Na3 b5 Bxf6 gxf6 Nd5 Bg7 Bd3 Ne7 Nxe7 Qxe7 c3 O-O O-O d5 Qf3 Be6 Nc2 f5 exd5 Bxd5 Qg3
trapLines.explanation: In the gxf6 variation, the balance is razor-sharp.

warningLines.name: d5 Permanent Hole
warningLines.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Ndb5 d6 Bg5 a6 Na3 b5 Nd5 Be7 Bxf6 Bxf6 c3 Bg5 Nc2 Rb8 a4 bxa4 Ncb4 Nxb4 Nxb4 O-O Bc4 a5 Nd5 Bb7 O-O Bxd5 Bxd5
warningLines.explanation: The d5 hole is the Sveshnikov's eternal weakness.

openings.name: Tarrasch Defense (Dubov)
openings.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 c5 cxd5 exd5 Nf3 Nc6 g3 Nf6 Bg2 Be7 O-O O-O Bg5 cxd4 Nxd4 h6 Be3 Re8
openings.overview: Dubov plays the Tarrasch Defense for its active piece play. The IQP on d5 gives Black open lines and dynamic possibilities. While the pawn is technically weak, Dubov uses the activity it provides to create attacking chances.
keyIdeas: Accept the isolated d5 pawn for active piece play
The d5 pawn controls c4 and e4, supporting piece activity
Develop actively with Nc6, Nf6, Be7 and use the open c-file
The IQP is a strength in the middlegame — push for activity before it becomes a weakness
traps: If White plays passively, the d5 pawn supports a powerful attack with pieces flooding the kingside
warnings: In the endgame, the isolated d5 pawn can become a liability — avoid trading too many pieces
Watch for Nxd5 captures — always verify tactics before they appear

variations.name: Classical Tarrasch
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 c5 cxd5 exd5 Nf3 Nc6 g3 Nf6 Bg2 Be7 O-O O-O Bg5 cxd4 Nxd4 h6 Be3 Re8
variations.explanation: The main Tarrasch line. Black develops actively and uses the IQP as a springboard for piece play. Dubov loves the dynamic nature of these positions.

variations.name: Swedish Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 c5 cxd5 exd5 Nf3 Nc6 g3 c4 Bg2 Bb4 O-O Nge7 Ne5 O-O Bg5 f6 Bd2 Nxe5 dxe5 Be6
variations.explanation: The Swedish Variation where Black pushes c4. This gives Black queenside space but the d5 pawn can become weak. Dubov finds creative resources in these structures.

trapLines.name: IQP Activity Explosion
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 c5 cxd5 exd5 Nf3 Nc6 g3 Nf6 Bg2 Be7 O-O O-O Bg5 cxd4 Nxd4 h6 Be3 Re8 Qb3 Na5 Qc2 Bg4 Rac1 Rc8 Qd2 Nc4 Qd3 Nxe3 fxe3 Qb6
trapLines.explanation: Black's IQP gives active piece play. The pieces flood to active squares.

trapLines.name: d5 Advance Trick
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 c5 cxd5 exd5 Nf3 Nc6 g3 Nf6 Bg2 Be7 O-O O-O Bg5 c4 Ne5 Be6 Nxc6 bxc6 e4 dxe4 Bxf6 Bxf6 Nxe4 Be7 Qg4 Kh8 Nc5
trapLines.explanation: White can exploit the IQP with a direct e4 push.

warningLines.name: Endgame IQP Liability
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 c5 cxd5 exd5 Nf3 Nc6 g3 Nf6 Bg2 Be7 O-O O-O dxc5 Bxc5 Na4 Be7 Be3 Re8 Rc1 Bf8 Nc5 b6 Nd3 Bb7 Nfe1 Nd7 f4 Nc5 Nxc5 bxc5
warningLines.explanation: In the endgame, the IQP becomes a target. White can siege it.

openings.name: Modern Benoni (Dubov)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 e6 Nc3 exd5 cxd5 d6 e4 g6 Nf3 Bg7 Be2 O-O O-O Re8 Nd2 Na6 f3 Nc7
openings.overview: The Modern Benoni is Dubov's most ambitious Black weapon against 1.d4. It creates an asymmetric pawn structure with clear attacking plans for both sides. Dubov thrives in the resulting complex middlegame positions where creativity matters more than pure calculation.
keyIdeas: Create an asymmetric structure with c5 and e6 against d5
The pawn on d6 supports a queenside pawn majority
Push b5 and a6-a5 for queenside counterplay
The Bg7 is a powerful piece controlling the long diagonal
traps: After d5 d6, if White plays f4 too aggressively, e5! or b5 creates strong counterplay
warnings: The d6 pawn can become a weakness — protect it carefully
White's central space advantage can be crushing if Black is passive

variations.name: Classical Benoni
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 e6 Nc3 exd5 cxd5 d6 e4 g6 Nf3 Bg7 Be2 O-O O-O Re8 Nd2 Na6 f3 Nc7
variations.explanation: The standard Benoni setup. Black aims for b5 and queenside expansion while White pushes on the kingside with f4-e5. The position is rich and dynamic.

variations.name: Fianchetto Benoni
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 e6 Nc3 exd5 cxd5 d6 Nf3 g6 g3 Bg7 Bg2 O-O O-O Re8 Nd2 Na6 a4 Nc7 a5 b5
variations.explanation: Against the fianchetto, Dubov still aims for queenside counterplay. The b5 push is the key break and creates an unbalanced position.

trapLines.name: b5 Queenside Counter
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 e6 Nc3 exd5 cxd5 d6 e4 g6 Nf3 Bg7 Be2 O-O O-O Re8 Nd2 Na6 f3 Nc7 a4 b6 Nc4 Ba6 Bf4 Bxc4 Bxc4 Nd7 Qd3 a6 a5
trapLines.explanation: Black's b6 and Ba6 create queenside counterplay.

trapLines.name: f5 Center Break
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 e6 Nc3 exd5 cxd5 d6 e4 g6 Nf3 Bg7 Be2 O-O O-O Re8 Nd2 Na6 Re1 Nc7 a4 b6 Nf1 Nd7 Ng3 f5 exf5 gxf5 f4 Nf6 Bd3 Ba6
trapLines.explanation: The f5 break opens the f-file for Black's rook and challenges White's center.

warningLines.name: d6 Weakness Exploitation
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 e6 Nc3 exd5 cxd5 d6 e4 g6 Nf3 Bg7 Be2 O-O O-O Re8 Nd2 Na6 f3 Nc7 a4 Rb8 Nc4 b6 Bf4 Nd7 Qd3 Nb5 Qb1 Nf8 Nd2 f5 exf5 gxf5 Nce4
warningLines.explanation: White's main target is the d6 pawn. If Black can't generate counterplay, the pressure becomes crushing.
